text: Last January, I made a pledge to stop buying anything I did not strictly need for twelve months.
The impulse to shop had become a persistent habit, and I wanted to confront it honestly.
The first few weeks were intense.
I would linger in front of store windows, resisting temptation.
I began to perceive my relationship with objects differently.
Most of what I owned was surplus — things acquired out of boredom rather than necessity.
By summer, I felt a profound sense of freedom.
I redirected the money I saved toward experiences: hiking trips, cooking classes, and long dinners with friends.
The experiment did not transform me into a minimalist overnight, but it shed light on a subtle truth.
It stems from connection, curiosity, and the patience to sit with discomfort until it passes.
